coredata.dc:title: Strain rate dependent plasticity of lithium-ion pouch cells: Experiments and simulations
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Tancogne-Dejean, Thomas
Grolleau, Vincent
Mohr, Dirk
coredata.dc:description: The safety of electric vehicles under crash event depends on the mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion cells under large deformation at high strain rates. Here, an extensive experimental campaign is performed on large-format pouch cells under out-of-plane indentation with two indenter shapes and speeds ranging from a few millimeters per minute up to ten meters per seconds, spanning six decades of strain rates. It reveals that the displacement at the onset of short circuit decreases with increasing strain rates, while a non-monotonic relationship is observed between the strain rate and the maximum force as well as the macroscopic cell tangent stiffness. Based on the experimental results, a phenomenological constitutive model is proposed making use of a Deshpande-Fleck yield locus and strain-rate dependent hardening. An internal variable is introduced to capture the softening at intermediate strain rates. The calibrated model is able to accurately reproduce the experimental data and is further validated on a hemispherical indentation performed at two meters per seconds.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion battery
Indentation
Plasticity
High strain rate
Finite element analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Displacement field analysis for slow indentation
Rough estimate of the stress-strain response
Summary of the material model parameters

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Dr. Christian Roth (ETH) for his help in performing the experiments. Dr. Juner Zhu (MIT) is thanked for valuable discussions. The support of MIT's Industrial Battery Consortium is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Micrograph of the undeformed structure of the Lithium-ion pouch cell, (a) full height and (b) close up view. ① Cathode with aluminum foil, ② anode with copper foil, ③ soft aluminum pouch.

Loading cases for the pouch cell: (a) hemispherical indenter and (b) cylindrical indenter. The positive tab is shown in black. The black cross indicate the indentation positions, while the displayed indenter position corresponds to the first experiment performed.
High-speed testing equipment: (a) Split Hopkinson Pressure Bar and (b) Direct impact.

Experimental results for the hemispherical compression at (a) slow speed, (b) intermediate speed, (c) validation speed and (d) fast speed. The full lines denote experiments on pristine cells while the dashed lines correspond to experiments on already shorted cells.

Experimental results for the cylindrical compression at (a) slow speed and (b) intermediate speed. The full lines denote experiments on pristine cells while the dashed lines correspond to experiments on already shorted cells.
Strain rate dependency of (a) the maximum force, (b) the displacement at the onset of short circuit and (c) the maximum slope.
Summary of the experimental results for the (a) hemispherical and (b) cylindrical compression. The repeats on shorted cells are shifted to match the displacement of the experiments on pristine cells.
Experimental results for the loading/unloading compression with hemispherical indenter compression at slow speed. The black lines corresponds to experiments performed on pristine cells.
Slope change during (a) the slow hemispherical indentation and (b) the slow cylindrical indentation.

Displacement of the cell outer surface during (a)-(c)-(e) the slow hemispherical indentation and (b)-(d)-(f) the cylindrical indentation. (a) and (b) shows the vertical displacement on the pouch surface at the instant of short circuit, while (c) and (d) highlights the vertical displacement of the closest surface point. (b) and (d) show the evolution of a line profile along the surface. The black lines represents the instant of short circuit.

Post mortem micrographs of the cells. The cut is made across the middle of the indentation along the long side of the cell. Cylindrical cells at (a) slow and (b) intermediate speeds. Hemispherical cells at (c) slow, (d), intermediate, (e) validation and (f) fast. The red arrows indicates the direction and end of shear bands crossing the entire sample.
Analytical fit: (a) force displacement curve and (b) the obtained stress-strain response.
(a) Isotropic Deshpande-Fleck yield surface with varying plastic Poisson's ratio and (b) two-stage rate independent hardening. The black lines indicates the calibrated model.
Effect of the internal variable evolution: (a) rate dependent, (b) no softening and (d) strain-rate-independent evolution.
Effect of the parameters for the strain rate dependent hardening: (a) D , (b) C , (c) m and (d) η .
Mesh of the lithium-ion cell with close-up view.
Strain and strain rate hardening surface for monotonic loading at constant strain rate
Simulation results for (a) the hemispherical indentation, (b) the cylindrical indentation and for (c) the loading/unloading experiment.
Equivalent stress field for (a) the hemispherical indentation and (b) the cylindrical indentation.
Slopes of the loading/unloading indentation from experiment and simulation.
Calibrated material model parameters.

head.ce:title: Strain rate dependent plasticity of lithium-ion pouch cells: Experiments and simulations

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Mechanical and Process Engineering
Swiss Federal Institute of Technology, ETH

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The safety of electric vehicles under crash event depends on the mechanical behavior of Lithium-ion cells under large deformation at high strain rates. Here, an extensive experimental campaign is performed on large-format pouch cells under out-of-plane indentation with two indenter shapes and speeds ranging from a few millimeters per minute up to ten meters per seconds, spanning six decades of strain rates. It reveals that the displacement at the onset of short circuit decreases with increasing strain rates, while a non-monotonic relationship is observed between the strain rate and the maximum force as well as the macroscopic cell tangent stiffness. Based on the experimental results, a phenomenological constitutive model is proposed making use of a Deshpande-Fleck yield locus and strain-rate dependent hardening. An internal variable is introduced to capture the softening at intermediate strain rates. The calibrated model is able to accurately reproduce the experimental data and is further validated on a hemispherical indentation performed at two meters per seconds.

ce:section.ce:para: The large deformation of Lithium-ion batteries contributes to the overall crash response of vehicles and can lead to short-circuit and thermal runaway. These critical events need to be considered in the design process of electric vehicles, which requires the development of accurate computational models for Lithium-ion cells and modules. During car crash and other accidental loading, the cells are subjected to loading at speeds of several meters per second. Rate-dependent constitutive models of Lithium-ion cells are needed to facilitate the design of safe vehicles with the help of finite element simulations.

Lithium-ion cells are composed of a stacking (“jelly stack”) of anode and cathode separated by a porous polymeric layer. The cathode consists of an aluminum foil coated on both sides with a lithium-based metal oxide powder, while the anode consists of a graphite coated copper foil. Both coatings are mixed with additives and held together with a binder. The electrolyte occupies around 40% of the cell volume. The jelly stack is typically packaged in the form of cylindrical, prismatic or pouch cells.

The large deformation response at low strain rate of Lithium-ion cells has been studied extensively and a review of experimental results and numerical modeling techniques can be found in [1]. Kisters and coworkers [2,3] performed experimental campaigns with speeds varying from a few mm/min up to 5m/s on elliptical, pouch and prismatic cells. Their study reveals that elliptical cells exhibit a higher maximum force with increasing loading rate, while pouch cells exhibit a continuous decrease of the maximum force. The maximum force in prismatic cells reveals a non-monotonic relationship with the loading rate, with a decreasing trend up to a strain rate of 4/s before increasing again at a strain rate of 40/s. For all cell types, the failure displacement decreases with the loading speed. The failure displacement is reduced by up to 40% from low to high strain rate indentation. The force-displacement response for pouch and prismatic cells changes drastically with increasing speeds with a transition from a convex to a linear relationship.

Similar results have been obtained through drop-tower experiments. Chen et al. [4] performed out-of-plane hemispherical indentations on 40Ah prismatic cells at speeds of up to 5 m/s. Xu et al. [5] performed drop tower compression on 18650 cylindrical cells and observed a stiffening of the cell with increasing strain rate. Xiao et al. [6] performed slow and high speed in-plane hemispherical indentations on 50Ah prismatic cells for a variety of lateral constraints. High strain rate tests performed at 2 and 3m/s exhibited higher stiffness than quasi-static tests. Post-mortem computed tomography revealed a combination of macroscopic buckling and shear band failure with more widespread and intense local damage in high speed loading. Zhu et al. [29]performed slow and high speed compression experiments on 234Ah prismatic cells in in-plane and out-of-plane directions. Their results show that the strain-rate hardening response differs depending on the loading direction. Pan et al. [7] performed impact experiments on small pouch cells according to the UN 38.3:2015 T.6 impact/crush test scenario at a speed of 3.46m/s with different State of Charge (SoC). An increase in force was observed for increasing SoC. Fras et al. [8] performed ballistic impact on small pouch cells with small caliber projectiles traveling at 360m/s.

Battery module studies have also been performed under high speed conditions. Xia et al. [9] performed impact loadings on battery modules composed of eight pouch cells, several cooling fins and an outer polymeric enclosure. Impact loadings up to 10m/s were performed for all three Cartesian directions using a hemispherical indenter, a cylindrical bar and a sharp wedge. Vastly different responses are observed depending on the loading direction. For out-of-plane indentation, the highest force levels are observed along with an increased risk of thermal runaway. For in-plane indentation, buckling of individual cells is reported with a low risk of thermal runaway. Kalnaus et al. [10] used a drop tower to perform out-of-plane compression experiments on an assembly of ten large format pouch cells at speeds of up to 0.7m/s. The displacement at failure decreased by about 60% over three decades of strain rate. Computed-tomography revealed that local failure of individual layers occurs at high loading speeds.

Numerical simulations are performed using either a homogeneous-equivalent or a detailed multi-layer model of Lithium-ion cells [1]. The strain-rate dependency is typically introduced using a Johnson-Cook term either on the homogenized media (e.g. [11–15]) or on the metallic foils and separator in the case of detailed models (e.g. [7,8]). Most of those studies are limited to the model calibration based on the results from one low and one high strain rate experiment. The strain rate dependency of the mechanical response of Lithium-ion cells is primarily due to the presence of the liquid electrolyte. Kisters et al. [2] and Zhu et al. [16] showed that dry cells exhibits a much lower strain-rate sensitivity than cells filled with electrolyte. In the case of high strain rate loading, a major contribution to the stress cell response is the electrolyte flow (or its absence) through the granular media. Zhu et al. [16] used poromechanics to model the electrolyte flow in a two-dimensional case and provided an analytical strain rate hardening expression based on Darcy's law. The model accurately captures the response of pouch cell coupons tested from 0.5mm/min up to 150mm/min.

In the present work, low (∼x223C 10−5 m/s), intermediate (∼x223C 10−3 m/s), and high speed (∼x223C 101 m/s) out-of-plane compression experiments are performed on large format pouch cells with a hemispherical and a cylindrical indenter. The deformation mechanism of the cell at slow speed is investigated using Stereo Digital Image Correlation (3D-DIC) while the surface temperature is monitored with an infrared camera. Micrographs of the deformed unit cell revealed a change in the failure mechanism with increasing strain rate. To model the strain-rate dependent behavior of the cell, a phenomenological plasticity model is developed based on a Deshpande-Fleck yield surface with an extension of the Johnson-Cook model including plastic-work based softening. Special attention is devoted to the strain-rate dependent behavior and to the observed softening at intermediate speeds. The model is calibrated on selected experiments and validated against intermediate speed indentations.

Commercially produced 37 Ah pouch cells with NMC/graphite chemistry are chosen. The in-plane dimensions of the cell (without the extra pouch length) are 270 mm × 110 mm and the thickness is 11.6 mm. Fig. 1 shows a micrograph of the characteristic jelly stack. The jelly stack is composed of 32 layers of anode, 31 layers of cathode and 64 layers of separator. The approximate thicknesses of the constituent layers are: ① 0.143 mm for the anode, ② 0.178 mm for the cathode, and 30 mm for the polymeric separator. The pouch skin ③ consists of a 0.168 mm thick soft aluminum film. All cells are fully discharged prior to testing, leading to an open-circuit voltage of around 3V.

The mechanical response of pouch cells is investigated under out-of-plane indentation. Three loading speeds are considered in this study and denoted hereafter as slow (0.7 mm/min), intermediate (72 mm/min) and fast (10 m/s). As a rough estimate (i.e. the loading speed divided by the cell thickness), they cover engineering strain rates from 10-3/s up to around 1000/s. More accurate estimates of the strain rates accounting for the non-uniform deformation fields below the punch will be used when identifying the plasticity model through FE-based inverse analysis (Section 4.3.2). An additional experiment with a particularly long loading pulse is performed with an impact loading speed of 2.2m/s. In order to investigate a possible stress state dependency, two types of indenters are used in the experiments: (i) a hemispherical indenter of 30mm diameter and (ii) a cylindrical bar of 30 mm diameter (see Fig. 2 ).

During the experiments, the open-circuit voltage of the cell is monitored synchronously with the force. The onset of short circuit is defined as the instant at which the voltage drops by one percent compared to the average value over the last 0.15 mm displacement interval (similarly to the technique reported in [3]). Due to the very low initial force, the zero point of the displacement measurements is set when the punch force reaches 20N during hemispherical indentations, and 50N during cylindrical indentations.

Slow speed experiments are carried out on a 250 kN electromechanical universal testing machine (Zmart.pro, Zwick). The indenter is screwed onto the moving cross-head, while the battery cell is placed onto the stationary (non-polished) steel base plate of the machine table. The loading speed is 0.7 mm/min corresponding to an average through-thickness strain rate of 10−3/s. Experiments are performed until a drop in force (typically associated with a drop in voltage) is observed. Due to the localization of damage within a narrow radius around the point of indentation, additional experiments are performed on a given cell after displacing the point of indentation by at least 70 mm. Due to the short circuit, the voltage evolution yields only meaningful results for the first indentation (pristine cell indentation). Tests performed on short-circuited cells (shorted cell indentation) are used to measure mechanical quantities only. For the hemispherical indentation, two cells are tested to detect possible cell-to-cell property variations. For all cells, the first experiment is performed with the indenter positioned as in Fig. 1, close to the positive cell tab. In order to evaluate the elastic properties of the cell material, an additional loading/unloading experiment is carried out. The experiment includes four unloading/reloading steps after an indenter displacement of 1.48 mm, 1.96 mm, 2.91 mm and 3.38 mm, respectively. The switch from unloading to reloading is made at a force of 0.2 kN.

Two 5MPx cameras (Grasshopper3, FLIR) equipped with 28 mm lens (Nikon 28 mm f/2.8 AI-s) are used to acquire images for stereo-digital image correlation (VIC3D, Correlated Solutions). The cameras were positioned such that a resolution of about 79mm per pixel is obtained. Images are recorded at a rate of 2Hz. The surface temperature of the cell is acquired at a frequency of 20Hz and resolution of 0.56mm/pixel using a high-speed infrared camera (X6801SC, FLIR) equipped with a 50 mm lens.

Intermediate speed experiments are carried out on the same universal testing machine at a constant speed of 72 mm/min, which is two orders of magnitude higher than the slow speed indentation. The same high speed infrared camera is used to monitor the surface temperature at an acquisition speed of 500 Hz, with a pixel size of 0.56 mm and a resolution of 511 × 399 pixels. Optical images are acquired using a high-speed camera (Photron SA-Z) equipped with the same lens as the cameras used for the slow experiments. Here, the acquisition frequency is also set to 500Hz, while the pixel size is 0.18 mm and 0.21mm for the cylindrical and hemispherical indentations, respectively. Due to the limited availability of cells for testing, only a single cell is used for each indenter type, leading to a single test on pristine cell for each indenter.

High-speed indentations of pouch cells are performed only using the hemispherical indenter. A Split Hopkinson Pressure Bars (SHPB) system is used for experiments at an initial speed of 10m/s, while a longer Direct Impact setup is used for the validation experiment with an initial speed of 2.2 m/s. The two systems are configured as follows:
•
The SHPB system features 30 mm-diameter AA7075 bars; it is composed of a 1.2 m long striker, a 2.9m long input bar and a 1.8 m long output bar (see Fig. 3 a). The strain gages are respectively positioned at a distance of 1.45 m and 0.2m from the input bar/specimen and output bar/specimen interfaces. An additional 75mm long aluminum bar (30 mm diameter) with one hemispherical end is positioned in series with the input bar. The impactor generates a 0.46 ms long loading pulse, resulting in a maximum displacement of about 4.6mm for a striker speed of 10 m/s. Standard formulas for wave transport [17] including a correction for wave dispersion (using DAVID software for SHPB post-processing) are used to estimate the force and velocity histories at the indenter tip and the output bar/specimen interface. After verification of the equilibrium, the force is computed only using the transmitted signal. Optical images are captured with the high-speed camera equipped with a 100mm macro lens (Tokina 100mm f2.8 1:1) at a frequency of 100 kHz to monitor the deformation of the cell.
•
The direct impact setup (Fig. 3b) makes use of two 40 mm diameter and 6m long AA6061 bars. In this configuration, the hemispherical extension bar is positioned in front of the output bar. The strain gage is glued onto the output bar at a distance of 0.40 m from the bar/extension interface. This system generates a 2.41ms long loading pulse which makes it possible to attain the failure displacements at lower speeds than the SHPB described above. The same camera and lens system is used in conjunction with Digital Image Correlation (VIC2D, Correlated Solutions) to compute the displacement of both bar ends; the force history at the indenter tip is computed based on the strain gage measurements using standard formulas.

We note that the back of the pouch cell was only supported by the flat output bar surface in all impact experiments. We also emphasize that the indenter moved in the SHPB experiments, while it remained approximately stationary in the direct impact experiment. Considering the good repeatability of the SHPB experiments, only a single direct impact experiment is performed.

In order to shed light on the failure mechanism of the cells at various speeds and with the two indenter types, micrographs are taken after failure of the cells. The cells are cut along the x-z-plane (see Fig. 1a for coordinate system definition) at the point of indentation using a metallographic cutting saw (Labotom 5, Struers).

The measured force-displacement curve and the cell voltage recording are shown in Fig. 4 (for the hemispherical indenter) and in Fig. 5 (for the cylindrical indenter). The curve label “pristine cell” refers to the first indentation on a given cell, while the label “shorted cell” indicates experiments on previously tested cells. The slow and intermediate hemispherical indentations (Fig. 4a-b) are characterized by an initially convex increase of the force-displacement curve at a constant voltage. A curvature change occurs before the force maximum. The voltage versus displacement curves suggest that a short circuit occurs as the force reaches its maximum. The force drop is steeper in the slow experiments (as compared to the intermediate speed), indicating a more stable damage process with increasing strain rate. At higher speeds (Fig. 4c and d), the force-displacement curves exhibit a first linear stage followed by a force plateau of around 12 kN and 15 kN for the indentations at 2.2 m/s and 10 m/s, respectively. No short circuit is detected during the fast tests (10m/s) where the force begins to decrease slightly after a displacement of 2 mm (Fig. 4d). For the test at 2.2 m/s (Fig. 4c), the onset of short circuit occurs at around 2.5 mm displacement, while the force remains more or less constant before decreasing steeply at around 4.7 mm. For all tests (including the one at 10 m/s), the voltage dropped to zero in the minutes following the experiment, indicating cell damage.

Quantitatively, increasing the indentation speed decreases the displacement at the onset of short circuit (Fig. 6 b) from about 4 mm (for the slowest case) to 3.6 mm (two decades faster) and to only 2.5 mm (fastest case). However, the maximum force evolves non-monotonically (Fig. 6a), decreasing by 40% (from 20.7 kN to 12.7 kN) over the first three loading speeds and increasing back to 16.2 kN for the fastest indentation. The maximum slope of the force-displacement curve is also computed (Fig. 6c). It is extracted from the approximately linear region of the force-displacement curves (Fig. 4). The maximum slope may be seen is a characteristic feature of the indentation response of batteries like the tangent modulus or hardening exponent of elasto-plastic materials. Similar to the maximum force, the maximum slope decreases from 7.4 kN/mm at slow speed to 6.5 kN/mm (12% lower) at intermediate speed and further down to 6.1 kN/mm (2.2 m/s) before increasing to 9.3 kN/mm for the indentation at 10 m/s. This indicates a non-monotonic behavior relationship between the loading speed and the large deformation response, prior to the cell failure.

Fig. 4 further reveals some important differences between experiments performed on pristine and already shorted cells. When repeating a test using a shorted cell, an offset in displacement is observed. While the offset is around 0.3 mm for the slow tests, it increases to about 2 mm for the intermediate and fast indentations. This offset is attributed to the delamination of the pouch skin during the first experiment, which leaves a small gap between the skin and the jelly stack that needs to be closed at the beginning of the experiments on shorted cells. When shifting the curves for slow and intermediate tests on the shorted cells horizontally, the obtained curves coincide with those for pristine cells (Fig. 7 a). The experiments on the shorted cells at speeds of 10 m/s exhibit a lower slope and plateau stress (around 12 kN) than those on pristine cells.

Cylindrical indentations at slow and intermediate speeds exhibit qualitatively similar results (Fig. 5). The force drops instantaneously in the slow experiments. The displacement at the onset of short circuit (and mechanical failure) is 40% (respectively 53%) smaller than that for the hemispherical indenters at slow and intermediate speeds. For the intermediate speed, the voltage drops around force maximum, but it comes back as the force decreases due to mechanical failure. The maximum slope decreases by about 30%, from 59 kN/mm at low speeds to 39 kN/mm at intermediate speeds (see Fig. 6). A summary of all monotonic force-displacement curves results to be used for the model calibration and validation is shown in Fig. 7. The observed non-monotonic trends are similar to the one reported for pouch cells [2,18] as well as for prismatic cells [3,13], which highlights that the strain-rate dependent response of the jelly stack is independent of the form factor.

Fig. 8 presents the results of the unloading and reloading when indenting a pristine cell with a hemispherical indenter. Small hysteresis loops are present during the unloading/reloading stage. The comparison of this result with the corresponding monotonic loading experiment reveals no differences in the loading response. The unloading slope increases as the indenter position at the beginning of unloading is increased (which implies an increase of the punch contact area). Table 1 reports the slope at the beginning of the unloading part. The slope increases almost linearly with the displacement at a rate of 94 kN/mm2.

In all experiments, no significant temperature increase is observed on the pouch surface prior to short circuit. After the onset of short circuit, the temperature rises slightly from room temperature to above 30°C in the vicinity of the indenter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Displacement field analysis for slow indentation
ce:section.ce:para: The slope of the force-displacement curve for the slow indentations is computed using a finite difference scheme. The corresponding plot (orange curve in Fig. 9 a) reveals an abrupt change in slope at a displacement of 2.4 mm. Initially the force evolution is convex, before it becomes approximately linear with a slope of around 7.4 kN/mm. Fig. 10 a shows the vertical displacement of the pouch surface at the instant of short circuit under hemispherical indentation. Two small bulges are visible in the vicinity of the indenter. The vertical displacement of the closest observable point to the indenter (blue dot on Fig. 10a) is shown as a function of the indenter displacement along with the force (black lines). Finally, Fig. 10e shows the displacement evolution along a line profile extracted along the long-axis of symmetry of the cell (blue line in Fig. 10a). The black line on Fig. 10e corresponds to the onset of short circuit. Up to a displacement of 2.5 mm, almost no vertical motion of the pouch skin is observed (Fig. 10c). After this point, the displacement near the indenter tip increases linearly with the indenter displacement at a rate of 0.9 mm/mm. The line profile shows an almost constant vertical displacement value, which suggests that away from the indenter contact area, the cell deforms in a uniform manner. In the close vicinity of the indenter, a small buckle is visible. This is tentatively attributed to the build-up of compressive hoop stresses that are reduced through the formation of a ring-shaped buckle.

Fig. 9b as well as Fig. 10b, d and f present similar results for the cylindrical indentation. The force evolution is first convex, reaching a maximum slope at a displacement of about 2 mm. After this inflection point, a slightly concave evolution takes place up to the fracture. No plateau or abrupt change in the slope are observed (Fig. 9b). The cylindrical indentation imposes a loading condition close to plane strain. In the absence of hoop stress, the battery folds around the indenter. Starting at 2 mm, a large bulging region forms near the indenter (Fig. 10f), up to a distance of 30 mm, leading to a rapid increase in the pouch skin displacement, i.e. at a rate of 1.7 mm per mm of indenter displacement.

Fig. 11 summarizes the micrographs after deforming the cells at different speeds. Fig. 11a,b include the results for cylindrical indenters, while Fig. 11c-f have been obtained after loading with a hemispherical indenter. One diagonal crack crossing the entire cell is observed for the slow cylindrical indentation, while two intersecting straight cracks are observed in the cell that was subject to intermediate speed loading. The planar cracks span the entire cell width, ultimately cutting it into two pieces. Moderate deformation of the layered structure is observed outside the fracture zone. The micrographs for hemispherical indentation (Fig. 11c to f) reveal different failure mechanisms. All samples show large deformation of the jelly stack layers below the indenter following the shape of the indenter tip. At low speeds (Fig. 11c), the liftoff of the cell is visible on the bottom surface, while a transition region with sheared layers is observed on both sides of the indenter. At the same time, a crack crossing the cell (see red arrow) is visible. Due to the axisymmetric loading, the cracks do not fully open and are thus less visible than in the experiments with cylindrical indenters. Similar mechanisms are observed for the failure due to intermediate speed loading (Fig. 11d), with two cracks and a kink in the layered structure at the bottom right. The micrographs for the two fast experiments (Fig. 11e,f) show a progressive failure mechanism: no straight cracks are visible, but the top half of the cell below the indenter is fully damaged while the bottom half is barely deformed. Here, many kinks are observed away from the indenter,

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rough estimate of the stress-strain response
ce:section.ce:para:
An analytical approximation is used to rapidly post-process the hemispherical indentations and understand the key features of the strain-rate dependent hardening. The formula is taken from previous work [19]. The deformation consists of the indentation under the punch and a variable transition radius outside. The vertical strain is assumed constant through the cell height and is related to the stress by a power law:
(1)
σ = A
ε
n

Minimizing the force as a function of the transition radius, an approximation of the force is obtained,
(2)
F = 2 π R H
(
δ H
)
n + 1
A
n + 1
(
1 −
1
2
n + 1
2
n 2
+ 3 n − 1
(
n + 1
)
(
2 n + 3
)
)
.
Here δ denotes the punch displacement; R and H correspond to the punch radius and cell height, respectively. According to this simple analytical model, the force is related to the displacement with a power
n + 1
. Fig. 12 a presents the analytical fit of the force-displacement curves at slow, intermediate and high speed. The parameters A and n are obtained through the minimization of the normalized mean square error. The identification is carried out up the inflexion point for the slow case and up to maximum force otherwise. The agreement is reasonable in the calibrated range. The estimated hardening exponent n decreases monotonically from 1.5 to 0.86 and 0.26, while the prefactor A decreases from 1215 MPa to 310 MPa and 154 MPa. The hardening law is convex at slow speed, while it is concave at higher loading rates (Fig. 12b). At the same time, the intermediate speed indentation exhibits an initially higher stress as compared to the slow case, but for strains greater than 0.12, the stress becomes smaller. The fast case presents an initial stress value of around 40 MPa.

A simple phenomenological model is proposed to describe the homogenized large deformation response of a stack of sheet separators and sheet electrodes (i.e. the representative volume element of the jelly stack comprised in a pouch cell). Due to the large thickness of the cell, the effect of the soft aluminum pouch is assumed to be negligible. While special attention is paid to the effect of strain-rate on the mechanical response, the effect of anisotropy is not addressed, thereby limiting the fidelity of the model to applications with out-of-plane loading (which is the most relevant loading condition from an industrial point of view).

In the sequel, vector notation is used to denote the components of the Cauchy stress tensor and the energy-conjugate strain increment tensor,
(3)
{ σ }
=
{
σ 11
,

,

,

,

}

}
T

The Deshpande and Fleck yield surface [20] (originally developed to describe the response of metallic foams) is chosen to approximate the boundary of the elastic domain for battery cells. Formally, we write
(5)
f =
σ
¯
( σ )
− k
where k is the deformation resistance and
σ ¯
is a pressure-dependent equivalent stress measure,
(6)

=
σ ¯

2
+
α 2
σ m 2

(

)
2

and
σ m
denoting the von Mises and mean stresses, respectively. The non-negative parameter
α 2
controls the shape of the yield surface. For
α = 0
, the von Mises yield surface is recovered. The equivalent stress can also be expressed through the general quadratic form

. The flow rule is chosen to be associated. As a result, the increment in plastic strain reads

As shown in [21], the work-conjugate equivalent plastic strain reads,
(12)

− 1

.

Fig. 13 a presents the shape of the yield surface for different plastic Poisson's ratios. For
ν p
= 0 ,
the matrix P is reduced to a diagonal matrix. In that case, the yield surface defines a sphere in
ℜ 6
. For isochoric plastic flow
(
ν p
= 0.5 ) ,
the von Mises yield surface is recovered.

The proposed yield surface is the same as the one used by advanced crushable foam models. However, instead of using a purely volumetric hardening model (with non-associated flow), we consider isotropic hardening model along with the assumption of associated plastic flow. As a result, the identification of a flow potential is omitted (and the number of model parameters reduced). Preliminary simulations of the slow hemispherical and cylindrical indentation revealed that the dominant stress states lie within a narrow range of stress triaxiality and Lode angle close to uniaxial compression, making it difficult to justify the use of an advanced crushable foam model. Instead of discussing the choice of the flow rule and the strain hardening definition, we will focus on the effect of the strain rate on hardening/softening.

The deformation resistance is dependent on the equivalent plastic strain
ε
¯
p
(strain hardening) and the strain rate
ε
¯
˙
p
(strain-rate hardening and softening). In the context of phenomenological modeling, the deformation resistance is additively decomposed into two terms describing the initial resistance
k 0
and subsequent hardening
Δ
k
. For each of these terms, a multiplier function is introduced to account for the effect of strain rate
(
λ 1
and
λ 2
)
. Furthermore, in close analogy with temperature-induced softening in conventional Johnson-Cook models, a softening term
λ ϑ
is introduced that depends on a (dimensionless) temperature-like internal variable ϑ . Formally, the deformation resistance is expressed in the form
(13)
k
[
ε
¯
p
,
ε
¯
˙
p
, ϑ ]
=
k 0
λ 1
[
ε
¯
˙
p
]
+
Δ
k
ε
[
ε
¯
p
]
λ 2
(
ε
¯
˙
p
)
λ ϑ
( ϑ )
.

Following the work of Wierzbicki and co-workers [22], the strain hardening term is often defined through a power law
(14)
Δ
k
ε
[
ε
¯
p
]
= A
(
ε
¯
p
)
n
.

In most studies (e.g. [14,19,22–24]), the hardening exponent n for battery cell stacks is around or larger than 1.5. This convex-shaped hardening law would lead to very high (and unrealistic) stress predictions at large strains. To remedy this issue, Li and Zhu [23] proposed a two-stage hardening law where an initially convex-shaped power law is followed by a concave-shaped exponential law. A similar two-stage approach is used here with a change of the nature of hardening at a critical strain
ε
c
. Enforcing C1-continuity of the deformation resistance, we employ a two stage hardening law of the form
(15)
Δ
k
ε
[
ε
¯
p
]
=
{
A
(
ε
¯
p
)
n
if
ε
¯
p
≤
ε
c
A
ε
c n
+ Q
(
1 − exp
(
− β
(
ε
¯
p
−
ε
c
)
)
)
if
ε
¯
p
>
ε
c
with the power law parameters
{
A , n
}
, the exponential law parameter Q, and the dependent parameter
(16)
β =
A Q
n
ε
c
n − 1

The two-stage hardening law is depicted in Fig. 13b. It features a convex-to-concave transition at the critical strain
ε
c
. The saturation stress is
k ∞
=
k 0
+ A
(
ε
c
)
n
+ Q
. The total of parameters to be specified are
{
k 0
,
ε
c
, A , n , Q
}
.

The strain rate dependency of the initial yield stress
k 0
is described through a first Johnson-Cook rate hardening term,
(17)
λ 1
[
ε
¯
˙
p
]
=
{
1 + D ln
(
ε
¯

ε

1
)
if

>

˙
1

ε
¯
˙
p
≤
ε
˙
1
with the transition strain rate
ε
˙
1
and the dimensionless parameter D. A second Johnson-Cook term is introduced to describe the effect of strain rate on the strain hardening term,
(18)
λ 2

1 + C ln

{

, C
}
.

The softening term takes the same parametric form as the temperature term in a standard Johnson-Cook model,
(19)
λ ϑ
( ϑ )
= 1 −
ϑ
m
with the model parameter m. Following the approach of Roth and Mohr [25], the temperature-like internal variable ϑ evolves as a function of the plastic work. Given an initial value of
ϑ = 0
, its evolution as function of the equivalent plastic strain history is prescribed by
(20)
d ϑ = η ω
[
ε
¯
˙
p
]

ε
¯
p
.

In (20), the rate of softening is controlled by the material parameter η . The function
ω = ω [
ε
¯
˙
p
]
defines a rate-dependent (smooth) switch from a regime without softening
( ω = 0 )
to a regime with intense progression of softening
( ω = 1 )
,
(21)

˙

≥

˙
3
where the length of the transition region is defined by the parameters
ε
˙
2
and
ε
˙
3
. The transition function is set at 0 after the critical strain εc.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Summary of the material model parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
The parameter
ε
˙
0
is set as the smallest strain rate obsreved in the experiment, Preliminary simulations revealed that the assumption of
ε
˙
2
=
ε
˙
0
and
ε
˙
3
=
ε
˙
1
leads to meaningful predictions, thereby reducing the total number of parameters to be identified to 13.

The effect of the strain rate softening on the model response is shown in Fig 14 . The reference case of no softening
( ω = 0 )
is shown in Fig. 14a. The full activation of strain rate-softening
( ω = 1 )
results in a reduction of the stress level for all three strain rates considered in this illustration. Using the smooth transition function over the range
10
− 3
/ s ≤
ε
¯
˙
≤ 0.2 / s
(Fig. 14c) then allows for the scenario where the stress level for the intermediate strain rate simulation (red curve) is lower than that for low strain rates (black curve).

Fig. 15 shows the effect of the parameters
{
C , D , m , η
}
on the strain rate dependent model response. The reference strain rate is set at
ε
˙
1
= 0.2
which implies that the intermediate strain rate is within the transition region. For that choice, the effect of changing the parameter D is mostly limited to the initial yield stress for the highest strain rate (Fig. 15a). The higher the value of D , the higher the initial yield stress. The parameters C and m have similar effects on the hardening (Fig. 15b and c); increasing either parameter leads to higher hardening for both intermediate and high strain rate. Lastly, the parameter η (Fig. 15d) controlling the conversion of plastic work into the softening internal variable shows an opposite effect with an increase leading to lower hardening.

Due to the liquid and granular material comprised in a battery cell, the extraction and testing of specimens that feature (macroscopically) homogeneous stress fields appears to be impossible with standard methods. In the present work, the material model parameters are thus identified inversely based on the results from the indentation experiments on pouch cells (as described in Section 3). The backbone for this identification procedure is a finite element model of the pouch cell.

Finite element simulations of the indentation experiments are carried out using the FE software Abaqus/Explicit. In order to reduce the computational cost, the simulations are performed on quarter cell models with symmetry boundary conditions. The cell is discretized using first-order hexahedral elements with reduced integration (C3D8R in Abaqus library), while analytical rigid surface models are used for the indenter and the flat supports (loading platens or Hopkinson bars). Preliminary FE simulations showed no influence of the choice of the geometry of the moving surface (flat or exact Hopkinson bar shape) on the results. The mesh with refinement towards the location of indentation is shown in Fig. 16 . The minimum element size is set to 0.55mm, while about 20 elements are chosen along the thickness direction of the cell. Mass scaling is used to reduce the computational time for the slow and intermediate speed simulations (with a minimum of 500,000 time increments to guarantee quasi-static equilibrium). The material model outlined above has been implemented in a user material subroutine (VUMAT) using a return-mapping algorithm [26].

The model parameters are calibrated in three steps:
1
The elastic constants
E = 4.2 G P a
and
ν = 0
are taken from the open literature (e.g. [19,27]). Their values are validated based on the simulation agreement for the unloading/reloading part of the experiment.
2
The plastic Poisson's ratio (i.e. the parameter describing the shape of the yield surface) and a first calibration of the rate independent hardening parameters
{
k 0
, A , n ,
ε
c
, Q
}
are obtained based on the results from the slow hemispherical and cylindrical indentation experiments. The cost function is the normalized error between the experimentally-measured and computed force histories,
(22)
Φ
=
1 2
∑
j = 1
2
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
F j
e x p
( i )
−
F j
s i m
( i )
F j
e x p
( i )
|
.
Here
F j
e x p
( i )
and
F j
s i m
( i )
denote the experimental and simulated force level for the ith data points (
i ∈ {
1 , . . . , N
}
); the index j indicates the loading scenario (hemispherical or cylindrical).
3
The remaining five parameters
{
C , D ,
ε
˙
1
, m , η
}
describing the strain-rate dependency of the model are inversely calibrated on the slow, intermediate and SHPB experiments. The calibration spans about six decades of strain rate. The cost function is defined in a similar manner as (22),
(23)
Ψ
=
1 3
∑
k = 1
3
1 N
∑
i = 1
N
|
F k
e x p
( i )
−
F k
s i m
( i )
F k
e x p
( i )
|
where
k ∈ {
1 , 2 , 3
}
indicates the loading speed (slow, intermediate, fast) of the experiment used for the calibration.

A preliminary study along with the calibration of other cells revealed that an almost constant strain hardening can be assumed, leading to
Q = 1 M P a
. Furthermore, the initial yield stress has a limited effect on the indentation response; it is thus chosen to be
k 0
= 0.1 M P a
which corresponds to the packaging pressure of the pouch cell. The minimization of the cost functions (22) and (23) is performed using a gradient-free Nelder-Mead algorithm [28]. The calibrated parameters are reported in Table 2 . The 3D plot in Fig. 17 presents the calibrated strain and strain-rate hardening law for monotonic loading at constant strain rate. It elucidates the unconventional nature of the hardening law that describes the homogenized rate-dependent large deformation response of the characteristic jelly stack of Lithium-ion cells.

The plots shown in Fig. 18 compare the predictions of the calibrated model with all experiments performed. The experimental curves have all been truncated at the point of maximum force since we are only concerned with the modeling of the large deformation response prior to severe damage and fracture. The agreement of the simulations (dashed curves) with the experimental results (solid curves) is good for all loading cases, in particular for those that have been used for model calibration. The model is capable to recognize the remarkably strong effect of the loading speed on the indentation response of Lithium-ion pouch cells (Fig. 18a). Furthermore, the model is able to predict the effect of the indenter shape with reasonable accuracy. It is emphasized that the experiment performed at 2.2m/s with the hemispherical indenter as well as the intermediate speed tests with the cylindrical indenter are true validation experiments that had not been exploited for model parameter identification. For all slow and intermediate tests, the proposed model does not capture the last 0.1mm of displacement where the experimental force-displacement curve becomes concave (probably due to the effect of internal damage).

Fig. 18c presents the simulation results of the experiments with unloading-reloading cycles. The unloading slopes are reported on Table 1. The numerical slopes increase linearly with the unloading displacement at a rate of 27.5kN/mm2. This increase is primarily related to the increase of the punch-specimen contact area. The first slope is accurately captured by the model, while the subsequent unloading slopes are slightly underestimated. Due the granular nature of the active material comprised in the cell stack, the elastic moduli are expected to depend on the volumetric plastic strain, which is a second-order effect that is not taken into account by the proposed constitutive model.

Selected equivalent stress fields from the simulations are shown in Fig. 19 at the point of force maximum. The highest stresses are found directly under the indenter irrespective of the speed of loading. At higher speeds, the deformation tends to localize near the indenter which is consistent with the experimentally-observed progressive failure mechanism in the high speed experiments (see Fig. 11).

Out-of-plane indentation experiments are performed on 37 Ah Lithium-ion pouch cells at indentation speeds ranging from about 10−5 to 10 m/s. A direct impact system has been designed to attain displacements of several millimeters at speeds of the order of 1 m/s. The experimental results exhibit a non-monotonic relationship between the loading speed and the maximum force, while a decrease by up to 40% in the displacement at the onset of short circuit is observed with increasing speeds. Similar conclusions are reached for both indenter shapes (hemispherical and cylindrical). 3D-DIC measurements reveal that the convex-to-concave change of the force-displacement response is associated with the formation of an outward buckle that surrounds the indented area. Micrographs of the cells also reveal a change in the failure mechanism at high loading speeds from forming straight through-thickness cracks to progressive damage of the multi-layer jelly stack structure.

A simple phenomenological model is developed to predict the large deformation response of battery cells in full vehicle crash simulations. It is emphasized that the apparent rate-dependent hardening response of Lithium-ion jelly stacks deviates substantially from that observed for common engineering materials. In addition to the convex-concave shape of the hardening curve, the strain rate effect is non-monotonic. Existing hardening models such as the Johnson-Cook model are unable to provide any reasonable engineering approximation. In addition, to the unconventional hardening, the plastic compressibility of the multi-layer material needs to be modeled. A Deshpande-Fleck yield surface with associated flow rule is employed together with a two-stage rate-independent hardening law. The strain rate sensitivity is incorporated using a combination of two Johnson-Cook strain-rate hardening term and along with a temperature-like internal variable that describes the strain rate softening. It is shown that the proposed constitutive model accurately predicts the force- displacement response of the Lithium-ion cells for both static and dynamic loading conditions.

The main novelty of the present work is a battery-specific constitutive model that is able to describe both strain rate hardening and softening. As a byproduct, a Hopkinson bar system for battery testing is presented along with original experimental results covering three distinct strain rate regimes. The present experimental and computational developments are made for pouch cells, but the procedures and models are expected to yield equally good results for cylindrical or prismatic cells.

Thomas Tancogne-Dejean: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Vincent Grolleau: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Dirk Mohr: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Dr. Christian Roth (ETH) for his help in performing the experiments. Dr. Juner Zhu (MIT) is thanked for valuable discussions. The support of MIT's Industrial Battery Consortium is also gratefully acknowledged.

J Power Sources
